signature: A cinephile whose collection is built on the American crime and institutional collapse canon — Tarantino, Fincher, Scorsese, the Coens, Villeneuve — who has expanded outward into class-satire with bite (Östlund's Triangle of Sadness and The Square), Sean Baker's marginal-America realism, and the procedural thrillers of Michael Mann. The taste prizes technical mastery and moral complexity above all, tolerates transgression only when it's in service of something — Aster's Eddington earns its place where Hereditary doesn't, The Substance where Hardcore Henry doesn't — and reveals a collector who is actively building toward canonical completeness across the 70s American paranoia cycle, European crime, and East Asian genre cinema.
prompt_section:
This curator's collection is anchored by: Sicario, The Godfather, Margin Call, Fargo, Interstellar, and Goodfellas. Prioritise films where procedural precision, institutional power, and moral collapse are inseparable — storytelling that makes you feel the weight of a system closing in on a person. Think: characters who understand exactly what they're doing and do it anyway.

Strong positive signals: crime films with architectural plotting (L.A. Confidential, No Country for Old Men, Heat); thrillers where information is weaponised (The Conversation, Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy, Zodiac); epics where scale serves character (There Will Be Blood, Apocalypse Now, The Deer Hunter, Once Upon a Time in America); class-satire with structural precision and dark wit (Triangle of Sadness, The Square — Ruben Östlund is now a gravitational director); Sean Baker's marginal-American neorealism (Anora, The Florida Project, Red Rocket); Michael Mann proceduralism (Collateral, Heat, The Insider — strongly underrepresented in collection); and international cinema with the same bone structure (City of God, Gomorrah, Cold War, Anatomy of a Fall, A Prophet).

Tone should lean toward: controlled menace or savage dark comedy, slow-build dread that pays off structurally, morally compromised protagonists with coherent internal logic, and restrained formal craft over stylistic excess. Transgression is welcome when metaphorically loaded (The Substance, Melancholia, Eddington) but not when it exists for shock value alone.

Avoid: pure horror and shock-escalation without narrative payoff (Hereditary banned). Avoid superhero adjacency even from trusted auteurs — The Dark Knight is MEH, Joker is banned. Avoid quiet domestic realism with no institutional hook (Minari banned, Power of the Dog MEH). Avoid slow-burn prestige that mistakes inertia for depth (The Assassination of Jesse James banned, Le Samouraï MEH). Avoid late-Scorsese bloat (Killers of the Flower Moon MEH, Silence MEH). Avoid maximalist auteur confections where style has consumed story (The French Dispatch MEH, Cosmopolis MEH). Avoid nihilism as endpoint (Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance banned).

Prioritise: precise, purposeful films where every scene is load-bearing. The watchlist reveals deliberate gaps: Michael Mann deep cuts, 70s American paranoia (All the President's Men, Three Days of the Condor, Network), Brian De Palma (Carlito's Way, Scarface), and the full Coen Brothers filmography. When in doubt, ask whether this film would sit comfortably between Sicario and The Social Network on a shelf.

gravitational_directors: Quentin Tarantino — 8 films in collection (Reservoir Dogs through Once Upon a Time in Hollywood as standard); the structural foundation
David Fincher — 6 films (Fight Club, Zodiac, Se7en, Social Network, Gone Girl, The Killer); procedural coldness is the register
Martin Scorsese — 5 films but late-period fatigue is real (Killers of the Flower Moon MEH, Silence MEH); Taxi Driver, Raging Bull, Goodfellas, Casino, Gangs are the valid Scorsese
Joel & Ethan Coen — 4 collection + 4 watchlist + 2 maybe; the full filmography is being deliberately mapped
Denis Villeneuve — 2 collection + 3 watchlist (Prisoners, Enemy, Incendies) + 2 standards (Dune I+II); strongest single-director loyalty
Paul Thomas Anderson — 2 collection (There Will Be Blood, One Battle After Another) + 5 watchlist; aspirational completism now converting
Ruben Östlund — 2 films (Triangle of Sadness, The Square); emerging gravitational pull around class-satire with structural precision
Sean Baker — 3 films (Anora, Florida Project, Red Rocket); sustained investment in marginal-American neorealism
Roman Polanski — 3 films (Chinatown, The Pianist, Repulsion); noir and psychological dread
Ridley Scott — 4 films but unreliable (Alien, Blade Runner, The Martian, All the Money in the World); early Ridley canonical, late Ridley tolerated then banned — Runner just banned
Michael Mann — 0 collection but 4 watchlist (Heat, Collateral, The Insider, Manhunter); the single largest gap-director
dominant_themes: Institutional power and its corruption — financial systems (Margin Call, Big Short, Wolf of Wall Street), criminal organisations (Godfather trilogy, Goodfellas, Casino, City of God, Once Upon a Time in America), political machinery (Spotlight, Munich, Judas and the Black Messiah)
Class and social stratification exposed through dark satire — Triangle of Sadness, The Square, The Big Short; Östlund's surgical dismantling of bourgeois self-image
Men who make irreversible choices and live inside the consequences — the Tarantino-Fincher-Coen axis
Procedural obsession — how things actually work, the mechanics of crime, espionage, journalism, capital; the pleasure is in the architecture

American mythology interrogated — the West (Hateful Eight, True Grit, No Country, Eddington), Hollywood (Once Upon a Time in Hollywood), the frontier dream (There Will Be Blood, The Brutalist, One Battle After Another)
Epic scale grounded in interiority — Dune, Interstellar, Apocalypse Now, 2001, Once Upon a Time in America
Marginal lives with moral dignity — Sean Baker's register; the collection accommodates tenderness when it's earned and unsentimental
preferred_tones: Controlled menace — threat present but not yet detonated (Sicario, Zodiac, Tinker Tailor)
Slow-build procedural dread that pays off structurally
Darkly comic class satire with structural rigour (Östlund, Coens, McDonagh)
Cold institutional register — Margin Call, The Conversation, The Social Network, Tár
Lyrical violence earned through context — Tarantino, Villeneuve, Coogler's Sinners
Unsentimental humanism — Sean Baker, The Holdovers, Anatomy of a Fall
formal_tendencies: Non-linear or structurally ambitious storytelling — Pulp Fiction, Adaptation, Oldboy, Rashomon (maybe)
Widescreen cinematography with purposeful negative space and earned stillness
Score as dread architecture — Jóhann Jóhannsson (Sicario, Arrival), Hans Zimmer (Interstellar, Dunkirk)
Dialogue-driven scenes where subtext carries lethal weight — Margin Call boardroom, Godfather negotiations, Glengarry Glen Ross (watchlist)
Restraint over excess — the camera earns its movements; stylistic maximalism tolerated only when content demands it (Kill Bill)
underrepresented: Michael Mann — Heat, Collateral, The Insider, Manhunter all watchlisted; the single biggest canonical gap given the procedural affinity
70s American paranoia cycle — All the President's Men, Three Days of the Condor, Network, The Parallax View all watchlisted or maybe; a deliberate archival project
Brian De Palma — Carlito's Way, Scarface watchlisted; Blow Out already in collection as bridgehead
Full Coen Brothers filmography — Blood Simple, Raising Arizona, Miller's Crossing, A Serious Man in maybe/watchlist
East Asian genre cinema — Memories of Murder (watchlist), Burning, The Wailing, The Handmaiden (maybe); strong appetite not yet converted to collection
French crime and European thriller — A Prophet (watchlist), Mesrine (maybe); wants the genre through entries other than Le Samouraï
Classical noir and paranoia — The Killing, Night of the Hunter, Witness for the Prosecution, Ace in the Hole, Rififi in watchlist/maybe
Alex Garland — Ex Machina and Annihilation both watchlisted; sci-fi as philosophical procedural
reject_patterns: Nihilism as endpoint — Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance banned; wants moral complexity, not moral void
Pure horror / escalating dread without narrative payoff — Hereditary banned, Longlegs banned; Aster accepted only when grounded in political or allegorical content (Eddington in collection)
Superhero adjacency even from trusted auteurs — The Dark Knight MEH, Joker banned
Quiet domestic realism with no institutional dimension — Minari banned, Power of the Dog MEH
Maximalist stylistic excess over story — The French Dispatch MEH, Cosmopolis MEH
Slow-burn that mistakes pace for depth — The Assassination of Jesse James banned, Le Samouraï MEH, All Is Lost MEH
Late-period auteur bloat — Killers of the Flower Moon MEH, Silence MEH, The Counselor MEH
Gore or shock as primary aesthetic — Hardcore Henry banned, Sympathy for Mr. Vengeance banned, Bone Tomahawk MEH
